name: Pitts Garnet Mine
short_description: Historic garnet mine near Minot offering rockhounders a chance to collect deep red almandine garnet specimens from this classic Maine locality.
description:
Pitts Garnet Mine is a historically significant garnet locality in Androscoggin County, Maine, representing one of the state's important mineral collecting sites. The mine area contains metamorphic bedrock rich in garnet-bearing schist and gneiss, characteristic of the New England uplands geology. Collectors visit this site to search for almandine garnet crystals, which can range from small 2-3mm stones embedded in host rock to occasional specimens several centimeters across. The property sits on relatively gentle, forested terrain at 275 feet elevation, making it more accessible than higher alpine garnet sites in the state.

This area is part of the Acadian terrane, formed during the Paleozoic collision of ancient continental plates that created metamorphic mineral assemblages throughout New England. The garnet here crystallized deep in the crust under high temperature and pressure, and has been exposed through millions of years of erosion. Historically, this locality has been known to Maine mineral collectors and appears in regional mining records, though commercial garnet extraction never reached significant scale here compared to industrial mines elsewhere.

Visitors should expect to search through weathered outcrop material and talus fields where garnets occur in the host schist. The red-brown color of almandine garnet makes it somewhat visible in the gray and tan metamorphic host rock, but careful examination is needed. Fresh exposures along old workings sometimes yield better specimens than surface collecting alone. The site's accessibility and proximity to Minot make it a worthwhile stop for New England rockhounders targeting Maine garnet.
history: Pitts Garnet Mine lies within the Acadian metamorphic belt of Maine, where Paleozoic collision between continental plates during the Ordovician to Devonian periods generated the high-temperature, high-pressure conditions needed to crystallize garnet throughout the regional schist and gneiss. Historical Maine mineral records document garnet collection at this locality, though it remained a small-scale prospect compared to industrial developments elsewhere in New England.
tips: Search the weathered schist outcrops and talus fields downslope from the old mine workings, where garnets weather slightly faster than the surrounding host rock and become visible. Look for deep red to brownish-red crystals, typically 2-5mm but occasionally larger, often dodecahedral in form. Bring a magnifying glass to identify garnets in the field and distinguish them from red feldspar. A cold chisel or small pry bar helps extract specimens from friable schist without damaging the crystals.
terrain_notes: The site sits at 275 feet elevation on a gentle slope within mixed deciduous and evergreen forest. Terrain is uneven with exposed metamorphic bedrock outcrops, weathered talus fields, and soft forest floor. Walking distance from parking is short to moderate, typically 0.1-0.3 miles to active collecting areas. Footing can be slippery when wet due to moss and leaf litter covering rocks; sturdy hiking boots are recommended.
best_season: May - October. Late spring through early fall offers the most comfortable collecting conditions when temperatures are mild and frost does not obscure outcrops. Summer months (June-August) can be warm with occasional afternoon thunderstorms in Maine; avoid after heavy rains when slippery conditions increase.
written_directions: From Minot center, take ME 119 east for approximately 1.2 miles. The Pitts Garnet Mine locality is accessed via a small dirt road or pullout area on the north side of ME 119 near the Androscoggin County line. GPS coordinates 44.10562, -70.333196 mark the approximate collecting area. Parking is limited; use a small pullout if available or park carefully along the road shoulder.
road_conditions: Paved highway access via ME 119; local access roads may be dirt or gravel and rough in wet conditions.
parking_notes: Limited informal parking near the site. Small pullout area available; capacity 2-3 vehicles. No developed parking lot.
collection_rules: Collect for personal use only. Respect private property boundaries if present. Remove only specimens; do not excavate large amounts of host rock. Leave the site better than you found it by replacing any rocks you turn over.
rules:
Personal use collection only
No commercial collection
Respect property boundaries
Minimize site disturbance
Remove loose material only; avoid heavy excavation
Pack out all trash
nearest_services: Minot, Maine, approximately 1-2 miles away, offers gas stations, small restaurants, and general stores.
accessibility_notes: Relatively easy terrain for most ages and abilities. Gentle slope with short walking distance to collecting areas. No wheelchair access due to natural terrain and outcrops. Children as young as 6 can participate with adult supervision. Sturdy footwear recommended due to uneven ground and slippery surfaces when wet.

faq.question: What kind of garnet can I find at Pitts Garnet Mine in Maine?
faq.answer: Pitts Garnet Mine is known for almandine garnet, the iron-aluminum variety that appears deep red to brownish-red in color. Specimens typically range from 2-5mm crystals embedded in metamorphic schist, though occasionally larger individual crystals are found. The garnets often display classic dodecahedral crystal forms.

faq.question: Is rockhounding free at Pitts Garnet Mine, and do I need a permit?
faq.answer: Yes, rockhounding at Pitts Garnet Mine is free with no permit required for personal use collection. This is a public access site near Minot where collectors can search for garnet specimens at no cost, making it an affordable option for Maine garnet hunting.

faq.question: How do I get to Pitts Garnet Mine from Minot, Maine?
faq.answer: From Minot center, take ME 119 east for approximately 1.2 miles. The mine locality is accessed via a small dirt road or pullout on the north side of ME 119. Parking is limited to a small informal area; you may need to use a road shoulder pullout.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to collect garnet at Pitts Mine in Maine?
faq.answer: The best season is May through October when weather is mild and outcrops are most accessible. Summer months offer warm conditions but bring afternoon thunderstorms; avoid collecting immediately after heavy rain when terrain becomes slippery. Spring and fall provide ideal temperatures and lower bug pressure.

faq.question: Is Pitts Garnet Mine beginner-friendly for rockhounding families with children?
faq.answer: Yes, Pitts Garnet Mine is excellent for beginners and families. The terrain is relatively easy with gentle slopes and short walking distances. Children as young as 6 can participate with supervision. No special equipment is required, though a magnifying glass and small pry bar help identify and extract garnets from the host schist.

faq.question: What tools and techniques work best for collecting garnet at this Maine locality?
faq.answer: Bring sturdy hiking boots due to slippery moss-covered rocks, a magnifying glass to identify garnets in the field, and a cold chisel or small pry bar to carefully extract specimens from friable schist. Search weathered outcrops and talus fields where red-brown almandine crystals stand out against gray metamorphic host rock. Look for characteristic dodecahedral crystal shapes, typically 2-5mm.

meta_description: Find almandine garnet at Pitts Garnet Mine near Minot, Maine. Free public collecting on metamorphic schist. Perfect for Maine rockhounders and families.
